notes: Roman province/region associated with Corinth and addressed alongside the Corinthian church in 2 Corinthians.
Capital of the Aksumite Empire; site of Frumentius's episcopal see; Christianity became state religion under King Ezana c. 330; major Red Sea trade hub.
Major Egyptian port city; intellectual and commercial center; seat of the Catechetical School; major Christian center from the first century onward.
Major base for the apostolic mission to the Gentiles.
Road station on the Appian Way where believers come out to meet Paul in Acts 28.
Ancient city in northern Mesopotamia; seat of an early Christian bishopric; the Chronicle of Arbela (disputed) claims a succession of bishops from the 1st century.
Roman Arelate in southern Gaul; site of the Council of Arles 314 addressing the Donatist controversy; attended by bishops from across the Western empire including 3 from Britain.
Roman province in western Asia Minor addressed in 1 Peter and associated with Ephesus.
Northwestern Anatolian port where Paul rejoins his companions in Acts 20.
Major Greek city visited by Paul in Acts 17.
Macedonian city associated with Paul's mission in Acts 17.
Traditional site associated in John with John the Baptist activity.
Village on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives associated with Mary, Martha, and Lazarus.
Fishing settlement associated with several early disciples in the Gospel narratives.
Roman province in northwestern Asia Minor named in 1 Peter.
Capital of the Roman province of Arabia; seat of Bishop Beryllus; site of the synod c. 244 where Origen corrected Beryllus's monarchian views.
Roman province; Christianity attested by Tertullian c. 200 and 3 bishops at Council of Arles 314; proto-martyr Alban.
Coastal city associated with Cornelius, Peter, and Philip the Evangelist.

City in central Cappadocia (modern Kayseri); seat of Bishop Firmilian c. 230–268; important ally of both Origen and Cyprian; Firmilian strongly supported Cyprian's rebaptism position against Pope Stephen I.
Galilean village associated with the sign at Cana and with Nathanael.
Galilean base of operations in early ministry.
Roman province in central Anatolia named in 1 Peter.
Major North African city; center of Latin Christianity from Tertullian through Cyprian and Augustine; seat of important regional councils.
Aegean island waypoint on Paul's southbound voyage in Acts 20.
Small island also known as Cauda, passed under shelter during the storm voyage of Acts 27.
Southwestern Anatolian harbor mentioned on the storm-driven voyage of Acts 27.
Phrygian city associated with the Epistle to the Colossians.
Major Achaian city associated with Paul and the Corinthian correspondence.
Mediterranean island associated with the Pauline mission to appoint elders in every town.
Mediterranean island visited on the first missionary journey in Acts 13.
Adriatic region named in 2 Timothy as Titus's destination.
City associated with Paul conversion and earliest post-conversion activity.
Lycaonian city named as the home of Gaius in Acts 20.
Euphrates garrison town; site of the earliest identified Christian house-church (c. 232 AD) with baptistery and wall paintings; destroyed by Sasanian siege c. 256.
Capital of Osroene in upper Mesopotamia; spiritual capital of Syriac Christianity; home of Bardaisan, Ephrem (after 363), and the Abgar/Addai tradition; site of the School of the Persians.
Roman province encompassing the Nile valley; home of the Alexandrian church tradition and early monasticism.
City in Judea; home of Julius Africanus who petitioned for its rebuilding as Nicopolis; identified with the Emmaus of Luke 24 in some traditions.
Major urban center in Asia associated with Paul long ministry and later Johannine tradition.
Cretan harbor where Paul's party debates wintering options in Acts 27.
City in Samaria known in the Roman period as Neapolis; birthplace of Justin Martyr. Now Nablus.
Region addressed in the Pauline letter to the Galatians.
Northern region central to Jesus ministry.
Roman province encompassing modern France; major region of second-century Christian expansion, centered on Lyons and Vienne.
Lycus valley city linked with Laodicea and Colossae in Colossians 4.
Malabar coast of southwest India; traditional site of the Thomas Christians; Pantaenus reportedly found Christians using Matthew's Gospel here (Eusebius HE 5.10).
City on the approach to Jerusalem in the Synoptic passion journey.
Temple city and climactic destination of the ministry narratives.
Coastal city associated with Peter's ministry in Acts 9-10.
Southern region including Jerusalem and the Judean wilderness.
City in the Lycus valley associated with Colossians and Revelation.
Roman London; Bishop Restitutus of London attended the Council of Arles 314; one of the earliest attested British episcopal sees.
Southwestern Anatolian province named on Paul's voyage in Acts 27.
Town on the coastal plain where Peter heals Aeneas in Acts 9.
City in Gaul (Lugdunum) where Irenaeus was bishop from c. 178; site of the 177 AD persecution under Marcus Aurelius attested in the letter preserved by Eusebius.
Lycaonian city where Timothy is encountered by Paul in Acts 16.
Island where Paul is shipwrecked and received hospitably on the voyage to Rome.
Port city south of Ephesus where Paul meets Ephesian elders in Acts 20.
Lesbian harbor city visited on Paul's route toward Jerusalem in Acts 20.
Lycian port city where Paul changes ships on the voyage to Rome in Acts 27.
Arabian city with early Christian presence; later site of the martyrdom of Christians by the Himyarite king Dhu Nuwas c. 523; trade route hub.
Home town associated with Jesus.
City in Pontus on the Black Sea; see of Gregory Thaumaturgus c. 240–270; major early missionary center in northeastern Asia Minor.
City named in Titus as Paul intended wintering place.
Major frontier city between Rome and Persia; home of Ephrem the Syrian until 363; seat of Bishop Jacob of Nisibis; ceded to Persia by Jovian's treaty of 363.
Western Cypriot city associated with Sergius Paulus in Acts 13.
Island associated with the composition setting of Revelation.
City of one of the churches addressed in Revelation.
City of one of the churches addressed in Revelation.
Macedonian city and Roman colony associated with Lydia and the Philippian church.
Cretan harbor preferred for wintering in Acts 27.
Central Anatolian region in the Roman province of Asia; origin of the Montanist prophetic movement c. 170 under Montanus, Priscilla, and Maximilla.
Roman province on the Black Sea named in 1 Peter.
Italian port where Paul finds believers on the approach to Rome.
Dead Sea site associated with the Yahad and the preservation of sectarian literature.
Italian coastal city touched on the voyage to Rome in Acts 28.
Imperial capital associated with the Roman church and Paul later ministry.
Eastern Cypriot city where Barnabas and Saul begin proclaiming in Acts 13.
Cretan cape named as a navigational point on Paul's voyage in Acts 27.
Central region between Judea and Galilee associated with Acts 8 mission activity.
Aegean island waypoint on the voyage from Chios toward Miletus in Acts 20.
Mediterranean island; site of Roman mines where Hippolytus and Pontian were exiled in 235 under Maximinus Thrax.
City of one of the churches addressed in Revelation.
Twin cities on the Tigris; capital of the Parthian and Sasanian empires; seat of the Catholicos of the Church of the East from the early 4th century onward.
Phoenician port touched on Paul's voyage under guard in Acts 27.
Black Sea port city in Pontus.
City of one of the churches addressed in Revelation.
Sicilian city touched on Paul voyage to Rome in Acts 28.
Macedonian city associated with Paul and the Thessalonian correspondence.
Road station south of Rome where believers meet Paul on the final approach in Acts 28.
City of one of the churches addressed in Revelation.
Northwestern Anatolian port visited by Paul in Acts 20.
Phoenician city where Paul finds disciples during the journey toward Jerusalem.

Ancient Armenian capital; site of the Mother Cathedral of Holy Etchmiadzin founded by Gregory the Illuminator c. 301–303 following the conversion of King Tiridates III; the first national Christian church building.
Region associated with John the Baptist.
Capital of the Himyarite Kingdom in South Arabia; Theophilus the Indian built a church here c. 354 per Philostorgius HE 3.4.
